question: Design a latency histogram system that tracks P50, P99, and P999 for each of 10,000 API endpoints receiving 100-100,000 requests per second. You must compute percentiles within 1% accuracy using under 1MB memory per endpoint, with all updates completing in under 10 microseconds. Which data structure do you use and how do you prevent the percentile snapshot read from blocking the hot write path?
answer: HDR Histogram: a fixed-size array of counter buckets indexed by log-linear encoding. Writers perform a single atomic fetch-and-add with no heap allocation or locking. Percentiles are computed by scanning O(buckets) cumulative counts, not sorting O(values). A sequence lock enables consistent snapshots without blocking writers. Memory is bounded at a few hundred KB regardless of volume. The trade-off is that raw values are discarded, requiring a separate sampling pipeline for exact logs.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  A["API Request Completes"] --> B["Record Latency in HDR Histogram"]
  B --> C["Compute Log-Linear Bucket Index"]
  C --> D["Atomic Fetch-and-Add on Bucket"]
  D --> E["Update Done in 50-200ns"]

  F["Dashboard Polls P999"] --> G["Snapshot: Read Sequence Lock Start"]
  G --> H["Copy All Bucket Counts into Local Array"]
  H --> I["Read Sequence Lock End"]
  I --> J{"Start and End Values Match?"}
  J -->|"No - Writer Concurrently Active"| K["Discard Copy and Retry Snapshot"]
  J -->|"Yes - Consistent Snapshot"| L["Scan Cumulative Counts to Find Rank"]
  L --> M["Decode Bucket Index to Latency Value"]
  M --> N["Return P999 to Dashboard"]
  K --> G

  O["Zero Latency for 10 Seconds"] --> P{"Histogram State"}
  P -->|"All Buckets at Zero"| Q["Cumulative Scan Returns Zero"]
  Q --> R["Dashboard Shows 0ms - Incorrect"]
  R --> S["Need TTL-Based Eviction or Heartbeat"]

  style D fill:#4CAF50,color:#ffffff
  style K fill:#ff4444,color:#ffffff
  style M fill:#2196F3,color:#ffffff
  style R fill:#ff9800,color:#ffffff
  style S fill:#ff9800,color:#ffffff
